ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to penne arrabbiata exists; 'pasta dish' (FOODON_03302050) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked pasta as a category; no specific arrabbiata sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for penne arrabbiata as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of penne arrabbiata weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with tomato sauce, cooked (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for penne arrabbiata exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked penne pasta and tomato-based pasta sauce, weighted to reflect a typical 60% pasta / 40% sauce composition. The input record's per_100g values (energy 157 kcal, protein 5.1 g, fat 5.1 g, carbohydrate 22.3 g) are consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor rounding. Sodium 214 mg/100g is plausible for a lightly salted preparation. All values cross-checked against USDA FDC entries #168930 (cooked enriched penne) and #170457 (tomato sauce, canned).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for penne arrabbiata exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'pasta, penne, white, boiled' (GI ~44–50, University of Sydney GI Database). The arrabbiata tomato sauce contributes negligible additional carbohydrate load and does not materially alter GI. GL per serving calculated as: (GI 50 × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (50 × 22.3 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) = 50 × 78.05 g / 100 ≈ 39.0 g GL units. GI boundary: ≤55 = low per University of Sydney classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 214 mg/100g; olive oil and tomato provide cardiovascular-protective compounds
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 214 mg/100g is in the low-to-moderate range; patients with hypertension should request no added salt during preparation and confirm the restaurant does not use high-sodium canned tomatoes, as a 350 g serving delivers approximately 749 mg sodium — about one-third of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 22.3 g/100g with low dietary fibre 1.7 g/100g; GI ~50 (low boundary)
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 350 g serving delivers approximately 78 g of carbohydrate with only 6 g of dietary fibre, producing a glycaemic load of ~39; patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request al dente cooking to reduce GI, and pair with a lean protein source such as grilled chicken to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat 0.9 g/100g; zero trans fat; olive oil provides monounsaturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is low at 0.9 g/100g and trans fat is absent; olive oil in the arrabbiata sauce provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and oleocanthal, making this dish suitable for cardiovascular disease patients when portion size is controlled and no additional cheese or cream is added.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli flakes) contain solanine which may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should monitor for joint flare after consuming this dish, as both tomatoes and chilli flakes are nightshade-derived; substituting the arrabbiata sauce with a pesto (basil, olive oil, pine nuts) eliminates the nightshade concern while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefit of olive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Penne Arrabbiata (also spelled Arrabiata or Arabiata) is a classic Italian pasta dish consisting of penne pasta — short, ridged, tube-shaped pasta made from durum wheat semolina — tossed in a spicy tomato sauce known as salsa all'arrabbiata. The sauce is prepared by simmering crushed or diced tomatoes with garlic, dried chilli flakes, and extra-virgin olive oil; the name 'arrabbiata' means 'angry' in Italian, referring to the heat of the chilli. The dish is vegan and halal by default, containing no animal products. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 157 kcal, 5.1 g protein, 5.1 g fat (of which 0.9 g is saturated), and 22.3 g carbohydrate with 1.7 g dietary fibre and 2.3 g sugar. Sodium is 214 mg/100g. The glycaemic index of white penne pasta is approximately 50 (low GI boundary), though the low fibre content means glycaemic response is not strongly modulated. Clinically, the dish is low concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to low saturated fat and moderate sodium, but moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to the high carbohydrate load per serving (approximately 78 g per 350 g plate). Tomatoes and chilli flakes are nightshade vegetables and may aggravate rheumatoid arthritis in sensitive individuals. This record was sourced from a GrabFood listing in Singapore where the dish appeared under a Turkish/Kebab restaurant category, but the dish itself is Italian in origin.
common_user_queries: How many calories in penne arrabbiata?
Is penne arrabbiata suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of penne pasta?
Is arrabbiata pasta high in sodium?
Can I eat penne arrabbiata with hypertension?
Is arrabbiata pasta good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for penne arrabbiata as a prepared dish exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC composite entries for cooked penne pasta and tomato-based sauce, weighted by typical composition. The input record's values are consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is estimated from the closest analogous University of Sydney GI Database entry for white boiled penne. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite pasta dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central as a single prepared-dish entry; values would require laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite preparation is not reported in the adapted USDA FDC entries used; estimated ~200 mg/100g from tomato content but not confirmed by a single approved source entry and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite restaurant preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FDC reference entries for plant-based pasta with olive oil and tomato sauce — no animal fats or hydrogenated oils are present in the standard arrabbiata preparation. 5. The input record listed this dish under Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma cuisine on GrabFood; the dish is Italian in origin and has been reclassified accordingly. Cultural tags have been corrected. 6. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entry for white boiled penne pasta; no direct arrabbiata composite GI entry exists in the database. 7. GL per serving = (50 × 78.05) / 100 = 39.0 (350 g serving × 22.3 g carbohydrate/100g = 78.05 g available carbohydrate).
